historical_summary: Germany invades Poland on September 1 1939, triggering British and French declarations of war. Polish resistance collapses within five weeks despite fierce defense. The campaign demonstrates combined-arms effectiveness against an outmatched opponent.
The Soviet Union invades eastern Poland on September 17 under the Molotov-Ribbentrop secret protocol. Soviet forces occupy roughly 200000 square kilometers and take over 200000 Polish prisoners.

Germany defeats France in six weeks through the Ardennes breakthrough. France signs armistice June 22. The BEF evacuates 338000 troops from Dunkirk but loses all heavy equipment. France's fall transforms the strategic balance of the war.

The Luftwaffe attempts to gain air superiority over Britain as a prerequisite for invasion. RAF Fighter Command prevails through radar-directed defense, Luftwaffe strategic errors, and unsustainable German losses. Hitler postpones Operation Sea Lion indefinitely.

Italy declares war on France and Britain on June 10 1940 as France collapses. Mussolini seeks territorial gains with minimal fighting. Italian military performance will prove consistently poor, creating strategic liabilities for Germany.

The US Congress passes the Lend-Lease Act on March 11, authorizing the president to transfer military material to any country whose defense is vital to US security. This formalizes massive material support to Britain and later the USSR without requiring immediate payment.

Germany invades Yugoslavia and Greece in April 1941 to secure the southern flank before Barbarossa and rescue Italy's failed Greek campaign. The campaign succeeds rapidly but may have delayed Barbarossa by several weeks.

Germany launches Operation Barbarossa on June 22 1941 with 3.3 million troops in 148 divisions. The largest land invasion in history achieves devastating surprise, destroying much of the Soviet air force on the ground and encircling hundreds of thousands of troops in the first weeks.

The Battle of Smolensk marks the first serious Soviet resistance and delays the German advance for two months. The diversion of forces south to Kiev is decided during this period, a major decision window that delays the Moscow drive.

The encirclement of Kiev produces the largest single capture of prisoners in military history. The diversion to Kiev is operationally brilliant but strategically controversial, consuming time before the autumn rains and winter.

The USSR evacuates over 1500 industrial enterprises east of the Urals between July and December 1941. This extraordinary logistical feat preserves the core of Soviet war production capacity despite the loss of western territory containing much of pre-war industry.

The German advance stalls before Moscow in temperatures reaching -30C. The Soviet counteroffensive beginning December 5 drives German forces back 100-250 km in places. Hitler issues a no-retreat order and assumes direct command of the Army. The war will not be short.

Japan attacks Pearl Harbor on December 7 1941. The US declares war on Japan December 8. Germany and Italy declare war on the US December 11. American industrial and military power now enters the war fully, transforming the strategic balance irreversibly.

Senior Nazi officials meet at Wannsee on January 20 1942 to coordinate the administrative implementation of the Final Solution. The conference does not initiate the genocide but systematizes the bureaucratic machinery for industrialized mass murder of European Jews.

Albert Speer replaces Fritz Todt as Armaments Minister after Todt's death in a plane crash on February 8. Speer's rationalization reforms will nearly triple German armaments output by 1944, though these gains are ultimately overwhelmed by Allied production and bombing.

Germany launches Case Blue, the 1942 summer offensive toward the Caucasus oil fields and Stalingrad. The operation splits into two diverging axes, overextending German forces. The drive toward Baku fails to reach the oil fields while the 6th Army becomes fixated on Stalingrad.

The US Navy sinks four Japanese fleet carriers at Midway, shifting the Pacific naval balance. Though primarily a Pacific event, it frees US resources for the Europe-first strategy and accelerates American shipbuilding mobilization.
The British 8th Army under Montgomery defeats Rommel's Panzerarmee Afrika at El Alamein, ending the Axis threat to Egypt and the Suez Canal. The Afrika Korps begins its long retreat westward.

Anglo-American forces land in French North Africa on November 8. Operation Torch opens a second front in the Mediterranean, traps Axis forces in Tunisia, and demonstrates Anglo-American amphibious capability. Germany responds by occupying Vichy France.

Operation Uranus encircles the German 6th Army at Stalingrad on November 19-23. Hitler forbids breakout. Goering's airlift promise fails catastrophically. The pocket surrenders February 2 1943 with 91000 survivors. Total Axis losses in the campaign reach approximately 800000.

Field Marshal Paulus surrenders the remnants of the 6th Army on February 2 1943. Of 91000 POWs, fewer than 6000 will return to Germany. Stalingrad marks the psychological turning point of the war and shatters the myth of Wehrmacht invincibility.

Goebbels delivers the Sportpalast speech on February 18, calling for total war. Despite the rhetoric, German economic mobilization still falls well short of British or Soviet levels. Full mobilization of women remains blocked by ideology.

U-boat losses spike to 41 boats in May 1943 (a 25% monthly loss rate), forcing Doenitz to withdraw from the North Atlantic. Allied escort carriers, long-range aircraft, radar, and Ultra intelligence have decisively shifted the balance. The tonnage war is lost.

The Battle of Kursk (July 5-23) is the last major German offensive on the Eastern Front. Soviet defensive preparations, informed by Ultra intelligence, absorb the German attack. The subsequent Soviet counteroffensives drive permanent westward momentum.

Operation Gomorrah creates a firestorm in Hamburg killing approximately 37000 civilians in the deadliest single bombing attack on Germany. Window chaff defeats German radar for the first time. Speer later says six more attacks like Hamburg could have ended the war.

Italy signs an armistice on September 8. Germany immediately occupies northern and central Italy, disarms Italian forces, and establishes a defensive line. The Italian campaign becomes a grinding attritional theater that ties down significant German forces.

USAAF raids on Schweinfurt ball-bearing plants in August and October 1943 suffer catastrophic losses (60 B-17s lost on October 14 alone, ~26% of the attacking force). Unescorted deep penetration bombing is proven unsustainable. Long-range fighter escort becomes the priority.

Roosevelt, Churchill, and Stalin meet at Tehran and agree on Overlord (May 1944 cross-Channel invasion) as the main Allied effort. Stalin commits to a simultaneous eastern offensive. The conference also discusses post-war Europe, including Soviet western borders.

Operation Argument (Big Week, February 20-25) targets German aircraft factories with 6000+ bomber sorties. Physical damage is significant but repaired within months. The decisive effect is attrition of the Luftwaffe fighter force, as P-51 Mustang long-range escorts engage and destroy German fighters at unsustainable rates.

The USAAF begins systematic attacks on German synthetic fuel plants in May 1944. By September, German aviation fuel output falls below 10% of pre-attack levels. This campaign is the most decisive strategic bombing achievement of the war, crippling the Luftwaffe and Wehrmacht more effectively than any other target set.

Allied forces land in Normandy on June 6 with approximately 156000 troops crossing the Channel on the first day. Despite fierce resistance at Omaha Beach, all five beachheads are secured. The western lodgment is established, opening the decisive second front in France.

Operation Bagration (June 23 - August 19) destroys German Army Group Center, inflicting approximately 400000 casualties. The Soviets advance 600 km, liberating Minsk and reaching the Vistula. It is the most catastrophic German defeat of the war in terms of forces destroyed.

The July 20 assassination attempt against Hitler fails. The subsequent purge eliminates much of the conservative military resistance. The regime radicalizes further, the SS gains power, and remaining institutional checks on Hitler's authority are destroyed.

Romania switches sides on August 23 following a royal coup. Germany loses access to Ploesti, its most important external oil source delivering approximately 2 Mt/year. The loss accelerates the fuel crisis and exposes the entire Balkan position.

Hitler launches the Ardennes offensive (Battle of the Bulge) on December 16 with the last German strategic reserves. The attack achieves initial surprise but fails to reach its objectives. The offensive consumes irreplaceable fuel, vehicles, and experienced troops.

The Soviet Vistula-Oder offensive (January 12 - February 2) advances 500 km in three weeks, reaching the Oder River 60 km from Berlin. German defenses in Poland collapse completely. Soviet forces now threaten the German heartland.

Germany surrenders unconditionally on May 8 1945. Hitler committed suicide April 30 in his Berlin bunker. Total German military dead: approximately 5.3 million. Total war dead in Europe: approximately 40 million including the Holocaust.
